speaker: interviewer
text: You and your husband and making Aliyah to Canada. And tell me more about your life here and whether the Holocaust and your experiences, both you and your husband, during the Holocaust affected the way you raised Yocheved. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we gave her-- we gave her more love, more just what we didn't have. Then we gave more. That gave us a lot in our life to show. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] She gave more-- she had more anything what we had. Things that we didn't have, would never want my child should not have it. When I came two years later-- my husband was here two years-- and I was in Israel two years with my child, with Eva. When we came here, the second day when I arrived I start working.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, here in Winnipeg was only one parish school. And the teachers they had-- not kindergarten, they didn't need it. But they needed a substitute if somebody was sick. I couldn't survive on that kind of money. And I was crying a lot. I want to go back to Israel. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And my husband had a-- a friend, the lawyer, Polack. He came to us, and from nerves what I was very nervous, I was drawing a lots. And when he saw my drawing he said, you have a lots of talent. I would take you to a place and show your drawing. He took me there. And-- and for sports you know, what they making on the jackets, all kinds of sports stuff. And they're making the designs from material there. And he saw mine and he said, you never had training? I said, no. I said, OK, you got a job. I was working there for a long time. What was the name of the place?

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name? I forgot already this place. Great West.

speaker: interviewee
text: Great West employer.

speaker: interviewee
text: Great West. I was working there for 10 years. For five years I was working. Then I went asking him for a raise. I should have more money. And he said, I can't give you like this. How come this girl get more money from me? And he said, well she have an invested. And I said, then I'm leaving. I was the best worker what they had. Then a half a day I went training there. A half a day, and he paid me for the whole-- the only thing I signed, I never leaving. That's-- I worked there all the years. And we tried my daughter to give anything what she wanted. The only thing we didn't give her, a brother or sister.

speaker: interviewee
text: She asked. She said, Mom, if you give me a brother or sister. I don't want to be the only one child. I'm going to be babysitter. I'm going to be everything. And I said, how can I think we have another child when I'm-- I'm the the bread-- I'm everything here? I made more money than my husband. And he went in in a store. He stayed in the store, and the store didn't have-- they moved away all the people from [INAUDIBLE]. They moved away all the people. They're building new homes, low rental. And I was the only one that needed to make the payments for everything. For the debts with my board-- $50,000 to make payments. And I told my daughter, how can I have a baby? She said, Mom, I'm going to be babysitter. I'm going to feed a baby. I'm going to-- well, you have to go in school.

speaker: interviewee
text: That-- I couldn't afford to be pregnant and to have another child. I went to my daughter. I tried all the best. Well, we want to sign for you going-- she should go in the Hebrew school. The only thing, I couldn't afford to pay. I came here to sign and I said-- that time they used to pay $5 each child, you know, each month. Government gave $5. This $5 I used to send to my mom. And they didn't want to accept my daughter if I don't have. I said, you're both working. Your husband's working and I working, you have to have-- to pay so much. I said, I don't have. I'm paying the tickets. I'm paying food with me paying the rent, and I don't even have a dollar left. The only thing what I can give you, the $5 what I'm sending to my mom, I'm going to pay you. And they said no. Then I was-- promised them, I stand up and I said to you, I promise you guys my daughter going to speak Hebrew better from you. And she going [SPEAKING YIDDISH] Hebrew. Only thing, a donation I would never give for the-- for the school and I'm keeping my word.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they did not accept her and you were very hurt.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was very hurt.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how did you teach her Hebrew?

speaker: interviewee
text: I take her to the public school. And in the house, we don't speak a different language, only Hebrew. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And my daughter kept phoning to me only Hebrew. We didn't want to speak a different-- not Jewish, not English. I said, we're going to learn English. We're going everything. I wanted you should know Hebrew. And with my daughters, you can hear her speak Hebrew, you would not believe it. She speaks Hebrew and her children are speaking Hebrew going in a Jewish school.

speaker: interviewer
text: So tell me, she finished school in the public school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, and she went to Ottawa University. She want to be a lawyer, and Ottawa-- Ottawa was the best. And they didn't take in any one student. You have to be [INAUDIBLE]. She went there for five years. And before she went, like, there, she met her husband and we gave her a trip to go to Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: She had a very good-- she finished the 12th grade, and she went to go there. And I promise you I'm going to give you-- And in England, there were five girls. And in England, in the airport she met-- there were five boys. Each boy said, that girl I wanted for me, you know? And her husband said, this girl is for me. They met. They went a trip.

speaker: interviewee
text: They went every place. They went to Romania. They went all around Italy, and then my daughter said, I am going to Israel, and he's going home. And she wanted to go to Israel to see the family life. And she never believed that she going to see him again. And here she was walking in [INAUDIBLE] in Israel and facing and she's coming on him.

speaker: interviewee
text: What are you doing here? And he said, I-- I was lonesome, and I decided to come to Israel. That's the way they met. And she came back, and she said, Mom, I want-- I want to go and to learn in Toronto. And the first thing what she said, I said what happened? You're having a boyfriend there? She went out with Jack Miller, if you remember. Yeah? And we liked the boy very much. He was in our home like a son. So you were surprised.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was surprised. She didn't want-- she-- she went to Toronto. In Toronto she went in and invested. And then she went to Ottawa. And he went in Ottawa, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your son-in-law?

speaker: interviewee
text: My son-in-law. And that's the way they finished and they married, and they moved to Toronto. They both--

speaker: interviewer
text: And what's his name? I forget.

speaker: interviewee
text: Erwin Weisz.

speaker: interviewer
text: And is he from--

speaker: interviewee
text: He's-- the parents is Hungarian.

speaker: interviewer
text: They survived the war together?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were survivors, too. They were in a concentration boat.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they're married and they live in Toronto and they have children, you said?

speaker: interviewee
text: They have two boys. One, Daniel. The older one is Daniel. The younger one is Jesse.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old are the boys?

speaker: interviewee
text: Daniel is going to be 17 in this summer, and Jesse will be 15 and 1/2. Daniel is very-- very beautiful. He's my-- I-- I should not tell. They're both-- if I have a headache and I'm sick and they put my-- their hand on me, I'm well. They're very good children. They give me so many beautiful on the phone and they have-- they don't call me grandma, no Safta and him Saba.

speaker: interviewer
text: Children should phone home, you know, how you feeling Safta? And the older one recognize right away how's your [INAUDIBLE]? [LAUGHS] And they tell you [INAUDIBLE]. The younger one, my husband learning a Ukrainian [INAUDIBLE], [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].

speaker: interviewee
text: They're both two beautiful kids. They're giving us so much. We wanted to live now. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we wanted so to be closer to them, and we decided to move to Toronto.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you'll be all together?

speaker: interviewee
text: For next year, I hope, in this time maybe in Toronto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Good luck.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to ask you whether you and your husband talk about your war experiences and the tragedies of the Holocaust together?

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband is telling me a lot. He's telling me from the war, and he's telling me what he went-- I don't never. I don't.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this is one of the first times you're talking about what you've been through.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's the first time. And I didn't think I'm going to remember so much, how much I told you. I spoke the first time in kibbutz. They want to know. Every child used to tell Friday. We used to tell everyone and they kept a record, you know. They wrote books for us. And that's the second time. I try to forget. I have lots of dreams.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me about some of those dreams?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have dreams where I'm drowning when I was in the water, when I couldn't breathe. I'm drowning. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Once I-- once I was in the bath, and I fall down from the bath, you know, like I was dreaming. Dreaming of running and I threw myself in the mountain up. I have so many dreams what I can't remember. Stuff what I'd never remembered and the dreams bring me to remember things.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what's your health been like over the years since the Holocaust?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when I was young, it was not so bad. I was healthy. I was working. Now when I've retired, I start, my headaches is worst and worst. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you think they're related to everything that you've been through?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: What makes you feel better? What takes the edge off the pain?

speaker: interviewee
text: Make me feel good when I'm with my daughter and with my grandchildren. I forget sometime I have headaches. And when I look on that, I say, well, it was worth it to survive. And thanks God, I have a good husband. He--

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me a little bit more about your relationship.

speaker: interviewee
text: I have a very good husband. If I had had a different one, I would not survive. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He doesn't-- he doesn't see not nobody better for me and nicer for me. [LAUGHS] I like him a lots. I think-- I think our love is even stronger now what we were younger. Younger, you're busy, always busy, you know? The baby was too small. We always gave a lot to the child what we never had-- now, we're both together. Yes, I think our trust is very strong.

speaker: interviewer
text: That sounds lovely.

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: It sounds lovely. It sounds very warm and comforting to you.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to ask you one final question. And that is if you had a message for your-- for your daughter and son-in-law, for your grandchildren, and for the rest of the world what would that be?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm just hoping never my grandchildren, all the kids should never see what I saw in mine age, what a war can bring, the worst thing. And I hope they will never, never see and not go through what we went through in a war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Amen.

speaker: interviewee
text: Amen.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about this picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taking summer camp in the Betar. We used to go for the camping. And the older kids was looking for the younger one. And we used to go for two weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: What country and what year would it be?

speaker: interviewee
text: That would in [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] '36 or '35.

speaker: interviewee
text: Something like that. I don't even remember exactly. If I don't tell you exactly, I don't remember. I know I was very small there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and this is in Kostopol?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in Kostopol, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Poland. And tell me the names of some of the people in the picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well there's my sister, my older sister Goldie. And my older brother, [? Lebel ?] And-- and me, Edna.

speaker: interviewer
text: Goldie's last name is Schweig?

speaker: interviewee
text: Schweig. And [? Lebel ?] Leon, [? Lebel ?] is Dimmerman.

speaker: interviewer
text: And anybody else from the family?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I-- I believe the middle brother, Meir, there, I believe.

speaker: interviewer
text: Meir Dimmerman?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And yourself. And at that time you were Edna Dimmerman.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Good. Picture taken? [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: '46, I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: 1946? '46 In [? Munchen. ?] And what's the name? Was all the kid survivors. Brought them together. We were there, and every child was almost sick. And they were looking after us near their camp. We couldn't go no place. There was the school. And this was Germany? Rosenheim, Germany.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Germany, in Rosenheim.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you know any of the people by name in the photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I know the name a lots, yeah. We were-- we were to get-- when we came there, every time they brought a couple kids, a couple kids, and that was a group of them, became like brothers and sisters. And when the first kids, eight children, and they-- they took us to Israel, we cried like-- like we will never see each other again. We thought we went through the same way like the wartime and separation, that we were scared.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do the last names of any of the people in the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't. I can't remember their last. But they're all married and they all don't have their names, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: They don't have their maiden names.

speaker: interviewee
text: Maiden names, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Married names. And you still visit with some of those people in Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah. Tora [? Farcus, ?] [? Tilda, Matilda ?]. A lots, funny names. You know. There's-- there's a girl what she came and she was-- couldn't go to sleep without the cross. They used to take away from her, you know, the-- yeah? They took away, and she used to go and make it from pieces of wood, a cross, and put it in there. She said that helped you. Thanks to that, she survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because she survived as a Christian person.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because she-- yeah. She's a red hair-- the girl there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, Matilda.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was Matilda.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she never knew your-- your real name. That's what they gave her, Matilda. And they called her Matilda. She never knew, and they never find nobody in her family.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she still lives in Israel and you're still in touch with her?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah. I'm still-- yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Good.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is a picture of you, Edna?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, that's in Rosenheim, where I came. They made in that picture how I looked very little. I look like a little girl for seven years, six years.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old are you actually in that picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: 10. 10, 11. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you want to tell us anything more about the time when that photo was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was very sick. So I cannot remember nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was the period in which you went into the hospital and had surgery.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I think I got-- thanks to the nurses what they-- they made a picture for me. And I have pictures from the nurses, too, you know? That's what I have, a little picture. They-- they needed for the little passport in the hospital to put it on-- on paper. Each paper, they need like a passport.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so this was your passport photo.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long after liberation was this? Days, weeks, months?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a year. Yeah, it was a year.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Which one is that? The Moshe.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your little brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my younger-- my younger brother, the baby brother. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Moshe Dimmerman?

speaker: interviewee
text: Dimmerman. Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where is this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: That taken in kibbutz in [? Inharut ?].

speaker: interviewer
text: This is in Israel already?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old was he and what year would this be? [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: 1948. 1948 or-- he came-- he came a lot later. I came sometime before the war in Israel. And he came already. It must be about 1948.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old would Moshe have been?

speaker: interviewee
text: That here-- maybe that picture's in Germany. That-- can I see that picture? That's not-- I just wanna--

speaker: interviewer
text: Come and take a peek over here.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, this in Germany, in Rosenheim.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right, in Rosenheim, Germany. And--

speaker: interviewee
text: In Rosenheim, in the camp. He must be here nine, 10 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so it's around the same time a couple of the earlier photos were taken.

speaker: interviewee
text: Something like that, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you get the pictures?

speaker: interviewee
text: That, my brother gave me. That brother of mine what he got killed--

speaker: interviewer
text: Meir.

speaker: interviewee
text: He had pictures. He had the pictures and all. Then I was left with all the pictures. Sometime I have double and sometime I have nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother gave me. He sent me pictures, you know? And that's what I got it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this is really early on after liberation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, liberation, yeah. Yeah, this picture is my brother Meir.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was it taken and where?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's taken in Kinneret.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Israel, [INAUDIBLE] Kinneret. I think this picture is made a month before he died.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was around 1948?

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like to say anything more about Meir? You've said a number of things on-- on the tape already.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he was very, very educated brother I had. [SIGHS] He wrote songs. He wrote the paper for Kinneret.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of-- he was the editor of the paper?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. Every week, Meir had a paper and that's-- he wrote it. I mean, you come in now in Kinneret, before you come in, there's a tank, what he--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where he was killed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Killed. And on the tank it said his name and a picture. And in Kinneret, before you come in, there's a big stone. They didn't forget.

speaker: interviewer
text: There's a big memorial in [INTERPOSING VOICES] tribute to him.

speaker: interviewee
text: Inside, yeah. And every year I go to Israel and I go there to remember him. I can never forget him. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is another family photo.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's another-- that's one is my brother-in-law, Bernard Schweig and my sister, older sister, with my mother. That's the day, I think, a week after she arrived to Israel from Poland. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And this is about-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: 1949, something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: About 1949, shortly before you were married. And your sister Goldie is in the center, and your mother is on the far side, and your--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you can recognize my mother. [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like to say anything more about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was-- that picture when we made it, I couldn't fully believe it. I was-- I was not very close to my sister and not very close to my brother-in-law. I was strange to them. You know, she remembered me good. Like she said, she would remember me even in the middle of the night. I-- I didn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were such a little girl when you were separated.

speaker: interviewee
text: It took-- it took a month. I got very close, and then I felt warm.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a special occasion that this was taken or--

speaker: interviewee
text: It's not a special-- it's just we took a-- I made a picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: They arrived?

speaker: interviewee
text: They arrived, and they came to my mother's room. We were sitting and here, I made a picture with my mom. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So who is this handsome man?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my husband. That's-- took the picture a month before we got married. He was very beautiful. He was so beautiful. He is so good. [LAUGHS] I think this good is better than the nice guys. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: He is a very handsome man, and that's about 1950.

speaker: interviewee
text: He's very-- yeah. And we got married. I just fall in love. I mean, you couldn't refuse. [LAUGHS] He was very good. He was good for my family. He used to bring the food, and he never had money. Believe me, when we got married we were in debts, so much money in debt, but he borrow money to bring food for my family.

speaker: interviewer
text: He's a very special man to you.

speaker: interviewee
text: He's very special. You can't get too many like that. It's something of single figures. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture made before my husband left Israel to Canada in 1956. My daughter, and me, and my husband.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you look dressed up, all of you.

speaker: interviewee
text: We always. We always. We never went different, not different, you know? We always was dressed. That's at the day-- [INAUDIBLE] made a picture and I took it out. My husband and we said, goodbye, you know? That is in my living room. You know, we made a picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of Rehovot [INAUDIBLE] in Israel. I was walking and they stopped me to ask if I would interest to go and model. And the first thing what I ask, how much money are you going to pay me? And it was not bad for hobby money. And I went in. I was working for them for two years. What was the name of the company?

speaker: interviewee
text: I've forgotten it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Here, here, here-- here I'm 20-- 20-- 25.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it's late '40s.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Late 1940s.

speaker: interviewee
text: '40s sometime.

speaker: interviewer
text: You look very well.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, my husband-- when-- when I used to go on Israel, you know, dressed-- all the cars used to stop with brakes, you know? Everybody thought I'm a model. Even I was not a model, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, you look wonderful.

speaker: interviewee
text: The first thing with me-- the first thing before food was clothes. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And you still like style, and clothes, and appreciate all those fine things?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have to take you up and show you my clothes, and you'll see.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's a deal.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's a deal.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you told me before something special about this picture for your husband.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture my husband liked it a lot. When they take this snap, you know. And my husband kept it in the wallet. And that's why it's so small. He cut it around. He kept it. That was his favorite picture. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is a picture when we came to Canada. Saturday, we used to walk in the park. And that was-- every Saturday that was our day. We took our daughter for a trip. And we walked in the park, and we made a picture. My daughter must be here 10, 11.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this is in-- in Winnipeg, Manitoba?

speaker: interviewee
text: Winnipeg in Manitoba.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it looks like it's Assiniboiune Park and it's the English gardens.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. That is there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were making some comments about your clothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] Well, I-- I was always dressed. My clothes, I could never afford to buy in Winnipeg [LAUGHS] at that time. And when I was walking with my daughter, everybody turned their head. She must be a tourist, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: And the clothes were made in Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I bought this in Israel. This is still from my modeling. I used to-- all my money I spend it all on more-- on clothes. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, it's a lovely picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my two grandchildren. On the left is Jesse. He's 13 years old. And the right is Daniel. He's 15.

speaker: interviewer
text: And these are the boys that make you feel better as soon as you hear their voice?

speaker: interviewee
text: That make me healthy, and I'm just living only for them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me a little bit about the boys? What they like to do and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, they have so much hobbies. They-- Jessie's a lot in electronic. And Daniel is-- is in a different, you know, he like to read a lot. And they don't watch too much television. My daughter doesn't give them. And they're looking special movies what Friday, that's the only day what they can watch. They're very kind, and they're so good. If my head hurt me, when they putting their hand, mine headache [LAUGHS] going away.

speaker: interviewer
text: They're like magicians.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I'm just surviving only to they come pretty soon, we're in closer to them.

speaker: interviewer
text: The left looks like a special occasion.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, this was a bar mitzvah to my older grandson, Daniel. It was the bar mitzvah in-- in Toronto, in an Orthodox shul. A very beautiful bar mitzvah-- you can-- it's like in a movie. [LAUGHS] And--

speaker: interviewer
text: And the year was 1993?

speaker: interviewer
text: And did he enjoy being kissed on each cheek?

speaker: interviewee
text: He-- only for Safta and for Saba. [LAUGHS] We lots of kisses. He said he got enough kisses for the whole year. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And it looks like it's a candle lighting ceremony.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, they-- they called-- they called everyone to light the candle. And that's what they called up us. We're lighting the candle and we kissed him to get elation. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And then the picture on the right?

speaker: interviewee
text: And right, that's my daughter, Eva, and-- and my-- my son-in-law.

speaker: interviewer
text: And his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Erwin Weisz. And which way-- I-- I don't know how-- he's a very good man. He is a very nice person, and he's a nice son to us. And we don't keep him-- we don't say he's a son-in-law, only we call him a son. As a matter of fact, he's calling Ima and him Abba.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he feels the same way about you.

speaker: interviewee
text: The same way, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it looks like they're also very dressed for an occasion?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's still-- this is the occasion to my granchild's bar mitzvah.

speaker: interviewer
text: The same grandson? Daniel?

speaker: interviewee
text: The same to then, yeah. I didn't take from Jesse's.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they look very happy.

speaker: interviewee
text: Very happy, they are very happy. And they are-- if he was not from Hungary and my husband was not-- my daughter would be not from Israel, I would thought they are the same, you know? They both--

speaker: interviewer
text: This is-- this is a very special picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: All my pictures are special. The only time when I'm painting and I'm relaxing when I feel not good, when I have the headaches. When I have the headaches, that's the only thing relax me. When I'm-- when I'm painting for my children, and I want to see my grandchildren, and I want to see them, I'm-- I'm sitting and I'm thinking, this picture I made it hoping when I'm pretty soon I'm going to be together with them. That's why I use these so much bright colors.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it's absolutely beautiful, and it's your therapy.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my-- well, that's true. When I'm depressed or everything, when something happens, I'm taking the little brush. I start painting, and that's relaxing me.
